coredata.dc:title: Chemical surface modification of carbon dots with Germanium: A highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence probe for Mg2+ detection using DFT and TD-DFT
coredata.dc:creator: Kiani, Samaneh
Taherkhani, Farid
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we chemically modified carbon dots (Cdots) using germanium, enhancing their sensitivity and selectivity as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for precise detection of Mg2+ ions. Excitation of the modified Cdots resulted in a distinct emission peak at 527 nm, enabling accurate measurement of Mg2+ concentrations across a linear range. The surface modification with germanium led to the creation of novel emitting centers on the Cdots' surface. Theoretical calculations using density functional theory (DFT) revealed the effective suppression of these emitting centers through a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process, utilizing the nitrogen lone pair electrons on the Cdots' surface. Furthermore, adjacent Cdots' surface pyrazino[2,3-f][1,10]-phenanthroline (PPT) nitrogen atoms formed chemical bonds with Mg2+ ions, resulting in the formation of aggregated Cdots. Consequently, the intricate complexation terminated the PET process, resulting in a novel emission peak at longer wavelengths. This study presents a highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescent detection system with exceptional low concentration detection limit for precise Mg2+ ion detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cdots
Germanium
Florescent
Chemosensory
DFT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structure of Cdot in two different views using B3LYP/6–31++G** method.
Cdot a) structure and Be b), Mg c), Ca d), Sr e), Ba f) complexes.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cdot and its complex.
Fluorescent spectrum of Cdot and its complex.
The mechanism of excitation and rest of [Cdot.Mg]2+ complex for fluorescent spectrum.
The mechanism of excitation and rest of [Cdot.Mg]2+ complex for fluorescent spectrum.
RDG images respectively for Cdot a), Be b), Mg c), Ca d), Sr e), and Ba f) complexes.
ET transfer parameters for the first five exactments for Cdot and Be, Mg, Ca, Sr and Ba complexes.
Charge transfers energy (Kcal.mol−1) from the lone-pair of N electrons to the internal Cdot orbitals. Before and after the presence of Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, and Ba cations.
Charge transfers energy (Kcal.mol−1) from the lone-pair of N electrons to the metals orbitals after the presence of Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, and Ba cations.

head.ce:title: Chemical surface modification of carbon dots with Germanium: A highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence probe for Mg2+ detection using DFT and TD-DFT

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Farhangian University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Farhangian University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we chemically modified carbon dots (Cdots) using germanium, enhancing their sensitivity and selectivity as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for precise detection of Mg2+ ions. Excitation of the modified Cdots resulted in a distinct emission peak at 527 nm, enabling accurate measurement of Mg2+ concentrations across a linear range. The surface modification with germanium led to the creation of novel emitting centers on the Cdots' surface. Theoretical calculations using density functional theory (DFT) revealed the effective suppression of these emitting centers through a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process, utilizing the nitrogen lone pair electrons on the Cdots' surface. Furthermore, adjacent Cdots' surface pyrazino[2,3-f][1,10]-phenanthroline (PPT) nitrogen atoms formed chemical bonds with Mg2+ ions, resulting in the formation of aggregated Cdots. Consequently, the intricate complexation terminated the PET process, resulting in a novel emission peak at longer wavelengths. This study presents a highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescent detection system with exceptional low concentration detection limit for precise Mg2+ ion detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the development of experimental techniques such as absorption and emission optical chemosensors and nano-chemosensors has greatly advanced the identification of metal cations, halides, anions, and biostructures. Among these analytes, magnesium ion (Mg2+) holds significant importance due to its adverse effects on ecosystems and human health, even at very low concentrations. In fact, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set a limit of approximately 1300 ppm for Mg2+ in public drinking water supplies. Understanding the charge transfer and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) processes in fluorophore-chelator interactions is crucial for determining the emission spectrum of a chemosensor. Recent progress in quantum computing has facilitated a better comprehension of these complex fluorescence emission mechanisms. By utilizing the electron-hole theory, the charge transfer mechanism in chemosensors can be accurately predicted, enabling the design and development of specialized chemosensors for specific applications. Hence, elucidating the charge transfer process and PET mechanism is vital for the prediction and design of chemosensors with targeted functionalities [1].

The electronic and optical properties of a germanium-incorporated carbon dot as a chemosensor for the selective detection of Mg2+ cations in aqueous solvents and the exploration of charge transfer and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) processes were investigated. The identification of such trace concentrations of this metal ion necessitates the use of highly sensitive and reliable sensing technologies. Traditional methods employed for measuring Mg2+ content include techniques such as atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry. However, these methods are often intricate, expensive, and time-consuming, leading to lengthy analysis times. The rapid and accurate detection of metal ions, including Mg2+, calls for the development of innovative, straightforward, cost-effective, and precise analytical protocols. In this regard, various fluorescent nanomaterials have been synthesized and explored as potential candidates for Mg2+ detection, encompassing metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [2], quantum dots (QDs) [3], nanoclusters (NCs) [4], polymer dots (Pdots) [5], and silicon nanohybrids [6].

The detection of metal cations represents a prominent and active area of research within the field of chemosensing. These efforts have led to the development of various types of chemical and biological sensors, including those utilizing ligands, metal–organic frameworks [7–9], and DNA derivatives [10]. These sensors are designed to detect toxic transition metals, such as Pb2+ [7,8,10] and Hg2+ [9,11], and are critical for environmental and health-related applications. Carbon dots (Cdots), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials [12], were serendipitously discovered in 2004 and have since garnered attention as highly efficient fluorescent nanoparticles with immense potential across various scientific disciplines. Notably, Cdots hold promise as chemosensors capable of electron transfer in the excited state for metal cation detection. The incorporation of germanium into the carbon dot structure imparts fluorophoric properties, enabling fluorescence-based detection. Fluorescence techniques offer remarkable adaptability, allowing the emission spectrum to be switched on or off at specific wavelengths based on the chemosensor type and the targeted ion. This is primarily due to their advantageous characteristics, including chemical inertness, excellent biocompatibility, high photochemical stability, and simplicity of synthesis. Moreover, the surface, composition, and morphology of Cdots can be easily modified to tailor their physical and chemical properties.

Various approaches, encompassing both top-down and bottom-up strategies, have been employed to synthesize Cdots. These approaches involve the incorporation of different elements such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, bromine, and selenium, which facilitate the modulation of their chemical and optical properties. Extensive literature supports the use of Cdots for selective fluorescence sensing of diverse metal ions, including Pb2+ [13], Hg2+ [14], Cu2+ [15], and Cr6+ [16], wherein the doping elements and surface chemical groups are modified. However, most of the reported fluorescent probes rely on single-intensity measurements, which pose challenges related to probe photobleaching, sample matrix light scattering, variations in excitation sources, and concentration-dependent effects. To create Ge-CDs as chemical sensors for cation detection, a straightforward and efficient method was introduced by Zhi Huang in 2015 [17]. However, certain modifications were implemented with the aim of integrating N-functional groups to establish metal-binding sites. This involved mixing citric acid with Ge-132 under specific heat conditions and adding AD2G [18] to the mixture, resulting in nitrogen doping in the CDs at the edges. Further steps in the procedure were conducted following the guidelines specified in reference [17].

The majority of these limitations may be circumvented by the use of technology, namely ratiometric fluorescence detection. This paper presents the first completely optimized ratiometric fluorescent probe for selective Mg2+ detection, which was carried out by the author. These peaks responded in the opposite way to Mg2+, which resulted in a visible change in the fluorescence spectrum. In order, in this study utilising quantum DFT and TD-DFT methodologies (Fig. 1 –a carbon dot structure). When a variety of metal cations were present in an aqueous solvent in 2013, JIN et al. used Cdot to successfully identify the Mg+2 cation with excellent selectivity. The theory of electron transfer was used to identify the molecular orbitals responsible for CT after looking at the various positions of the Mg2+ cation on the ligand.

Using the DFT and exchange correlation methods, the B3LYP/6–31++G** basis set [19], and the ground state, serrated Cdot was optimized in both the water solvent and the water molecule. The involvement of Mg2+ in the accumulation of Cdot and the emergence of the red emission peak was further investigated using theoretical simulations. First, using DFT [20] and TD-DFT [21] techniques, the optical characteristics of a number of germanium-doped aromatic-based compounds with an amino group joined to a pyrazino[2,3-f][1,10]-phenanthroline (PPT) molecule were examined. A nineteen-aromatic-ring structure with four germanium atoms and a surface PPT group was chosen with PPT [22]. The similar technique was used to optimize its complexes with the second group's elements, including Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, and Ba. A straightforward Cdot's structure is seen in Fig. 1 from two different perspectives. Germanium elements cause Cdot's structure to be deformed and out of its planar state. The ability of this Cdot to combine with these cations close to the teeth is evident in the following. Moreover, NBO calculations using the same methodology were carried out to examine changes in the ligand's internal electron transfers before and after the complex was formed. These calculations can be used to determine the electron exchanges in these complexes between cations and Cdot. It was determined how the density of states (DOS) diagram had changed and how much the energy gap had changed [23].

The electron localization function is a further measure that signals the development of these complexes and displays changes in electron density in the ligand well (ELF). This value actually shows the likelihood that an electron with the same spin will be found around the reference electrons. The electrons of the valence layer and the nuclei have been clearly distinguished by ELF, which visually displays the electron density and improves comprehension of the system's electrical characteristics. The reduced density gradient (RDG) approach can be used to look at the bonds created in the complex. In actuality, the average reduced density gradient (aRDG) approach, which is described in equation (1), is a generalization of RDG.
(1)
R D G
r
=
∇ ρ
r
ρ
r
4 / 3

Equation (1) is the electron density at distance r in equation (1). Drawing RDG on sign(λ2)ρ which, in fact, represents a form of critical point in chemical bonds and can be used as a useful parameter to determine the kind of bonds, reveals that bonds have a certain type. Selecting the proper calculation technique for the excited state of the chemosensor is crucial. To examine this nano-chemosensor, the computation technique and basis set CAM-B3LYP/6–31++G** are a good approximation. In excited state electron transfers, the CAM-B3LYP approach is more accurate than the B3LYP method [24]. By using this technique in an aqueous solvent, absorption and optical emission spectra for Cdot and complexes were obtained. To ascertain the PET process prior to and following the complex, the electron balances during absorption and emission were examined. Charge transfer from chelators to fluorophores in various states was made possible by the electron-hole theory, which was also used to create the PET process. The ability of electron-hole theory to excite CT is very strong. Only electron exchanges between MOs with coefficients greater than 0.00001 were taken into account in CT computations. All calculations were done using Gaussian 09 package and its data was obtained via Gausview 05, Multiwfn 2, and VMD softwires [25].

The spectrum of alkaline-earth element complexes and UV–Vis Cdot is shown in Fig. 3 . As the compound formed, two indicator peaks with a minor difference in wavelength and intensity appeared on Cdot. These alterations are slightly more pronounced for the complex containing magnesium, though. The peak had a redshift of approximately ΔAbs = 19 nm and occurred with less intensity in the region between λAbs = 376 nm in Cdot and λAbs = 395 nm in the [Cdot.Mg]2+ complex. These transitions π → π* and n → π* are altered by altering the Cdot geometry as a result of the intricate creation of energy levels. The primary goal of this research is the selectivity in the fluorescence emission spectrum, which can also be used in the form of a kit, even if the change of 19 nm in the absorption wavelength indicates that this nano-sensor is ideal for magnesium cations.

The PET procedure is to blame for the Cdot's incorrect emission spectrum. The hole-electron theory allows for highly accurate investigation of the ET process. It was discovered that there is no PET transition only for the complex containing Mg after looking at the first 5 states of Cdot excitation and all studied complexes. We only have an emission wavelength of λEmi = 527 nm for this compound as a result. Fig. 5 displays the heat map of these transitions together with the numerical results of these calculations. Red orbitals stand in for electrons, whereas blue orbitals stand for holes. The left chelator, the carbon dot, and the right chelator are the three parts of the nanosensor that make up the heat map. It is simple to see that the heat map and the hole and electron orbitals have a direct and obvious relationship. The descriptors of the charge transfer process are the integral parameters of the overlap of electron and hole orbitals (S) and the separation between the electron and hole centres (D) shown in Table 1 . Charge transfer happens when D has a large value and S has a small value [26].

A strong ET has occurred in Cdot (nstate = 1), per the findings shown in Table 1 (nstate = 1). ET does not exist after the complex with Mg has formed. According to ET for nstate = 1, the Cdots emission spectrum was turned off by the PET process, which was triggered by ET from the chelator → fluorophore. Due to the geometric change of Cdot ET from chelator to fluorophore once the combination with magnesium is formed, it is lost and brightens the fluorescence emission in the [Cdot.Mg]2+ complex. An ET has also occurred in other compounds, and this exception for magnesium cation has made it selectable [27,28].

One of the most effective ways to demonstrate electron transitions in the ground state is through NBO analysis. The transitions made in the Cdot nano-chemosensor before and after the complex with cations were formed can be examined using the findings of the NBO study. Table 2 shows the electron transfers from N non-bonding electron pairs to surrounding bonds (Cdot internal bonds) before and after adding the examined cations to the water solvent. In comparison to electron transfers to * levels, nitrogen electron pair transfers have a higher value. As shown in Fig. 1 and 2, the general geometry of Cdot has not altered significantly as a result of the cation addition and complex creation, and the emission spectrum for the Cdot has been established. The engagement of nitrogen LPs with vacant Mg orbitals is connected to the Mg complex. The value of charge transfer from the nitrogen electron lone-pair to σ*C61-C60 is 11.02 Kcal.mol−1, and it has drastically decreased following complexation. For instance, the electron transfer from LP σ*C55-C56 is lowered to 8.29 from 14.21 Kcal.mol−1.

Table 3 displays the NBO computations' outcomes. According to the data, a number of electron transfers, including N LPs → LP*Mg, have occurred after the complex has formed. The majority of these transitions are created by LPs electrons. For instance, the value of the LP transition → Mg is 43.09 Kcal.mol−1. Just the electron pairs of the N62 and N63 atoms have been used in the charge transfer process from the electron pairs of the Cdot, which is because magnesium is located close to these two atoms. Moreover, Table 2 displays the Cdot's intramolecular electron transfers. These transitions are close to the teeth that make up the Cdot complex. These transitions contain LP transitions as well as σ* and π* transitions. The intensity of these transitions reduces with the addition of magnesium and the creation of a complex because this mechanism is disturbed.

The nature of chemical, physical, and van der Waals bonds in complexes can be displayed using an RDG plot on sign(λ2)ρ. Fig. 7 displays RDG pictures for Cdot and complexes on the sign(2) plane. These images suggest that two sigma bonds only develop after the [Cdot.Mg]2+ complex forms, as illustrated in Fig. 7–c. The modifications are as if the sigma bond is not created and are similar to Cdot in Fig. 7–a in the area enclosed by a red circle for other complexes. Moreover, these bonds are described in a different fashion in the pictures associated with ELFs in (S2) such that the pairs of nitrogen electrons are inclined towards Mg. A useful visual aid, in particular for evaluating the electrical behavior of the valence layer, is the ELF electron density. The electron density pictures of the ELF Cdot and complexes are displayed in Figure (S2). The geometry has changed as a result of the complex's development, and the altered electron transfers are clearly seen in the NBO analysis and shifting energy gaps.

Using quantum DFT and TD-DFT methodologies, Cdot has been studied as a chemical sensor in this work to find magnesium cation in aqueous solvent. Since Cdot can bind to the magnesium cation in two identical sites, the complex was optimised in both locations. The geometry of the ligand was altered as a result of the formation of two sigma bonds between PPT Cdot and the Mg cation. NBO analysis and electron density images clearly showed the effects of these geometric alterations. The ligand's UV–Vis spectra showed a red-shift of Abs = 19 nm after the complex was formed. The PET procedure throws off the Cdot emission spectra in aqueous solution, but the [Cdot.Mg]2+ complex's synthesis brightens it at the Emi = 527 nm wavelength. This ligand has a relatively high ET of chelator and fluorophore in an aqueous solvent, which ceased once the complex was formed and brightened the emission spectra, as demonstrated by electron-hole theory. This ligand can be employed as a nano-chemosensor with excellent selectivity to detect magnesium cation by colorimetry and spectroscopy since it has the capacity to brighten the emission spectrum as a result of complex formation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photo-induced electron transfer of [C 60+ Abacavir] nano-complex and feasibility of C 60 fullerene application as a chemical shift reagent: a DFT/TD-DFT insights
